cards.text: Ellipsis in C++ is denoted by {{c1::three dots (...)}} and allows functions to accept a {{c2::variable number}} of arguments of {{c3::any type}}.
To declare a function with ellipsis: {{c1::returnType functionName(type param1, ...);}} where ellipsis must be the {{c2::last parameter}}.

cards.question: What C standard library function famously uses ellipsis?
cards.answer: printf() - formats and prints output with variable number of arguments based on format string
cards.explanation: printf uses ellipsis to accept any number of arguments. The format string determines how many arguments to expect.

cards.front: What are the main problems with ellipsis parameters?
cards.back:
• No type safety - compiler can't check argument types
  • No way to know how many arguments were passed
  • Requires manual argument parsing with va_list macros
  • Easy to cause undefined behavior with wrong types

cards.text: To access ellipsis arguments, use macros from <cstdarg>: {{c1::va_list}}, {{c2::va_start}}, {{c3::va_arg}}, and {{c4::va_end}}.

cards.question: What's dangerous about this printf call?
cards.code:
int x = 42;
printf("%s", x); // Oops, used %s for int
cards.answer: Undefined behavior! %s expects a char* but got an int. This could crash or print garbage. Compiler may warn but can't prevent it.
cards.explanation: Ellipsis provides no type safety. The format specifier must match the argument type exactly or you get undefined behavior.

Instead of ellipsis, use type-safe alternatives:
  • Function overloading for known variations
  • Variadic templates (template parameter packs)
  • std::initializer_list for same-type arguments
  • std::vector or array for dynamic number of arguments
These provide compile-time type checking and are safer.

cards.front: When should you use ellipsis in modern C++?
cards.back: Almost never. Only use ellipsis when interfacing with C libraries that require it (like printf). Use variadic templates or other type-safe alternatives instead.

cards.text: {{c1::Variadic templates}} are the modern C++ replacement for ellipsis, providing {{c2::type safety}} and {{c3::compile-time}} checking.

cards.front: What are the requirements for using ellipsis parameters?
cards.back:
• Must be the last parameter in parameter list
  • Function must have at least one named parameter before ellipsis
  • Need some way to know argument count/types (sentinel value, count parameter, or format string)

cards.question: Why is std::initializer_list better than ellipsis for variable arguments?
cards.code:
// Ellipsis version (unsafe)
void sum(int count, ...);

// Initializer list version
void sum(std::initializer_list<int> nums);
cards.answer:
std::initializer_list provides:
  • Type safety (all elements must be int)
  • Automatic size tracking (no count needed)
  • Compile-time error checking
  • Iterator support for easy traversal
cards.explanation: std::initializer_list knows its size and enforces type at compile time, while ellipsis has no type checking and requires manual size tracking.
